text: And so The Last of Us season 2 stumbles
and flops to its conclusion like a fat
guy trying to pull vault in the middle
of a storm of fan backlash, cratering
audience scores and viewing figures that
HBO conveniently doesn't talk about
anymore. In short, this season has been
nothing short of a complete and total
disaster. A boring, bleak, depressing,
badly written slog of a show that
actually strained the very limits of my
endurance just to get to the ends. And
I've watched the acolyte. We were on the
brink of extinction.
And the thing that's so sad about this
whole thing is that this disaster was
entirely predictable. In fact, I
predicted it months before the season
even started in this video right here.
But man, even I couldn't have imagined
just how bad it was going to be. Apart
from a couple of halfdecent action set
pieces where all that HBO money was on
full display and a performance from
Pedro Pascal who was clearly doing his
best to carry the entire show on his
back. Season 2 has been absolute trash
from start to finish. Boring when it
should have been compelling, laughable
when it should have been poignant,
frustrating when it should have been
exciting preachy self-indulgent and
pretentious. It's everything the game
was but dialed up to 11. And now that
it's finally over and we've had the full
season 2 experience, I think now is as
good a time as any to reflect on where
exactly The Last of Us went wrong. So,
grab your potato masher and let's
explore the top five reasons this show
went off the rails. Problem number one,
Joel. It's no secret that killing off
Joel in the first few hours of the game
has gone down in history as one of the
worst narrative decisions that anyone's
ever made ever.
[Music]
Well, I said one of the worst, and you'd
think that five years of fan backlash,
alternative suggestions, and endless
video essays would have convinced Neil
Drman to do something a bit different
this time around. But nope, there it is.
Almost beat forbeat, exactly like in the
game at the same exact point in the
narrative. And as it turns out, it was
just as unpopular now as it was 5 years
ago. Not only did killing off the
protagonist of the first season in such
a pointless, brutal manner leave a sour
taste in people's mouths, but it also
left a gaping hole in the show that no
other character could possibly fill. The
relationship between Joel and Ellie was
the beaten heart of both the game and
the show. And without it, The Last of Us
lost that vital element that kept people
engaged, its humanity. And there's
really no getting around this. Pedro
Pascal was by far the best thing about
The Last of Us.
gay. Yeah, he's arguably overhyped and
overexposed as an actor, and I'm still
not convinced he was the best man for
the job, but one thing I can't deny is
that the guy can [ __ ] act. Pascal
brought some muchneeded authority,
gravitas, and believability to the show.
And with him out of the picture, it was
up to problem number two to carry this
show. Which brings me neatly along to
problem number two, Ellie. There are
certain unavoidable truths that have to
be reconciled in life, dear viewer. One,
true happiness is something that you
only recognize once it's gone. Two, Aith
is objectively the better girlfriend for
Cloud in Final Fantasy 7. And three,
Bella Ramsay was the wrong [ __ ]
choice to play Ellie. Now, I'm not here
to [ __ ] all over her appearance or hate
on her as a person because that's pretty
unfair and mean-spirited. She's just an
actor doing a job. And believe it or
not, she actually comes across as pretty
likable and downto-earth in interviews.
But the harsh reality is that not every
actor can play every role. They have to
physically embody the character they're
playing. And if they can't do that, they
at least have to capture the essence of
that character's personality. Clearly,
Ramsay doesn't look anything like Ellie.
So, the first option is right out the
window. But she also doesn't have the
acting range to embody Ellie either. Her
performance ranges from so wooden you
could build a log cabin out of it to so
over the top that it makes Nick Cage
look like Anthony Hopkins. None of it
feels believable. She's laughable when
she's trying to be intimidating,
obnoxious when she's trying to be
likable, and weirdly disconnected when
she's trying to be tender and
vulnerable. Much as I dislike the game,
one thing I can't deny is that Ashley
Johnson [ __ ] crushes it as Ellie. And
comparing some of their most iconic
scenes, you can't help but notice the
massive gulf of talent between her and
Ramsay. It's a lethal combination that
absolutely kills your investment in the
character. And it doesn't help that
she's been written as a completely
different person from the game. Game
Ellie was a broken woman for most of
part two, completely consumed with
revenge to the point where it takes a
visible toll on her mind and body. Yeah,
it was depressing, but it at least made
sense, and it perfectly fit with the
theme of revenge, ultimately costing
both parties everything. TV show Ellie
is completely different, though. You can
practically feel the insecurities of the
writers shining through, where they try
so hard to make her tough and resilient,
where she used to be open and trusting,
and instead of being strong and
determined, she just comes across as
prickly, arrogant, and aggressive. Her
relationship with Dena has gone from
teetering on the brink of collapse to
some idilic teen romance where they're
cracking jokes, smiling, laughing, and
[ __ ] like they don't have a care in
the worlds. They never really argue or
fall out. They see everything pretty
much the same way, and they're both so
frustratingly agreeable that you have to
wonder why Dena even exists. It makes
zero [ __ ] sense, and it leaves you
with this horrible nagging feeling that
Joel's death really didn't make much of
an impression on Ellie at all. Problem
number three, the pacing. The pacing of
Last of Us season 2 is absolutely
abysmal. In the games, this was less of
a problem because the player was in
control most of the time and cutscenes
only came up occasionally to move the
story along. Here, though, that's
literally all we've got. And it doesn't
take you long to realize that there
really isn't much of a story to tell
here. And so, a good chunk of the season
is just timewasting, busy work [ __ ]
How many times do we have to watch Ellie
and Dena wandering through a forest or a
deserted city, swapping [ __ ] jokes and
pointless banter that tells us nothing
about them or moves the plot forward or
builds up the worlds? How many tedious
sex scenes did they have to cram in that
feel completely out of step with two
people in a tense survival situation
where death can come at any moment?
Yeah, there's nothing like being
freezing wet and cold and surrounded by
zombie fungus monsters to get you in the
mood. There were whole episodes where
nothing of any significance happens and
I was bored out of my mind and it all
feels like such a waste of potential.
It's the hallmark of self-indulgent
hubris on the part of the showrunners.
The belief that they're producing such
artistic perfection that the audience
will just luxuriate in every second of
it. It's a show about mushroom zombies,
mate. Get a [ __ ] grip. Problem four,
the writing. Everyone in this show is a
[ __ ] idiot. From Ellie and Dena just
casually riding down the middle of a
city street that could be crawling with
enemy troops to the wolf leaders who
decide to occupy a hospital that's
infected with airborne virus spores that
could infect them at any moment to Joel
and his complete refusal to stand up for
himself or explain his past actions to
Abby walking off and leaving someone
who's clearly going to come after her
for revenge later. Nobody's actions make
any [ __ ] sense. It's all just done in
service of the plot. And that's not even
counting the dialogue that's so cringe
inducing it feels like even an AI would
refuse to spit it out.
I'm going to be a dad.
Yeah.
[Music]
Come on, guys. That couldn't have even
sounded good in your heads. Problem
five, the message. Yeah, you probably
knew this bit was coming, and for good
[ __ ] reason. The game was notorious
enough for being aggressively
pro-current thing, but the show takes it
to such a ludicrous degree that it
actually becomes laughable. The whole
100 pound actress beating up a bunch of
accommodating stunt men routine has been
done so many times that I'd become kind
of numb to it by this point. But then
we're treated to the insane sight of
Bella Ramsay, who looks like a small
child at the best of times, literally
overpowering a man who's at least twice
her size and many times her strength.
What are you [ __ ] Who the [ __ ] do
you think is going to buy something like
this? Like seriously guys, did your
fight choreographer not put up his hand
at some point and say, "Uh, I don't
think this is working. I don't think
people are going to believe this." But
the girl bossing doesn't end there, dear
viewer. Not by a long way. I can pretty
much guarantee that almost every
position of command and authority is
going to be held by a woman in this
show, and every military unit will be at
least 50% female. Because as we all
know, putting women of childbearing age
on the front line is definitely a smart
move when the human race is teetering on
the brink of
extinction. And I'm not kidding. There's
even a point where a heavily pregnant
woman is somehow allowed to go out into
hostile territory like she's not going
to be a complete liability to herself
and everyone around her. Son of a
[ __ ] What the [ __ ] is wrong with you?
I mean, do you really think that anyone
in their right mind believes this
nonsense for 1 seconds? The inconvenient
truth is that if The Last of Us actually
happened for real, women would never be
allowed anywhere near combat or danger
in general for that matter because
they're too valuable to risk losing.
They wouldn't be allowed out on patrol.
They wouldn't serve in military units,
and they probably wouldn't be in
positions of frontline command either.
They would be at home having babies and
keeping things running behind the lines
while the men took all the risks and
made most of the big decisions. Is it
glamorous? No. Does it fit with modern
progressive politics? Also no. But
funnily enough, all that stuff tends to
go out the window once people start
dying and all the safeguards and
comforts of modern society get stripped
away. You really would be amazed how
quickly the idea of girl bossing dies
out when people have to take actual
physical risks and put their lives on
the line. On the other hand, the male
characters around them are usually
portrayed as corrupt, bigoted, or
cowardly at worst, and at best benignely
agreeable and progressive, always happy
to take insults and disrespect without
ever giving anything back and defer to
their betters. Because again, that's how
guys like Neil Drman in their
comfortable 21st century progressive
bubble imagine that the world would
look. And in a show that's so desperate
to be taken seriously, it's amazing how
much The Last of Us relies on clichéed
tropes that became outdated and
laughable half a decade ago. It's a show
born from bad source material, adapted
into a preachy, self-indulgent mess by a
man whose activism was always more
important than storytelling, repeating
the same mistakes he made with the game,
but still expecting a different result.
The Last of Us season 2 really was the
most predictable of disasters. They knew
what they were heading towards. They had
every opportunity to change course and
do things better this time, but instead
they just plowed on. Too arrogant to
listen and too zealous to change. Well,
you really did get what you deserve, and
The Last of Us deserved the failure it
created for itself. I'm just [ __ ]
glad I don't have to watch any more of
it for a couple of years. Anyway, that's
all I've got for today. Go away now.
